single-1.title: Purpose of the LENGTH() function
single-1.prompt: What does the LENGTH() function return when used on a text column in SQL?
single-1.hint: It counts something about the text.

help.concept: LENGTH() returns the count of characters in a string, not words or rows.
help.hint_1: Think about how many letters or symbols are in the text.
help.hint_2: LENGTH() is about the size of the text value, not the table.

multi-1.title: Which are valid string functions in SQLite?
multi-1.prompt: Select all functions below that are valid string functions in SQLite.
multi-1.hint: Some functions change case, others extract parts of text.

help.concept: String functions operate on text values. SUM() is an aggregate numeric function, not a string function.
help.hint_1: Look for functions that work with text, not numbers.
help.hint_2: UPPER, LOWER, SUBSTR, and LENGTH are all for strings.

fill-1.title: Function to extract part of a string
fill-1.prompt: Which function extracts a substring from a text value in SQLite?
fill-1.hint: Focus on the missing SQL concept rather than the exact missing word.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the SQL term that matches the job the statement is trying to perform.
help.hint_1: Think about what the missing part is supposed to do in the statement.

fill-1.template: To get the first three letters of a region, use ____ (region, 1, 3).

sketch-1.title: What are string functions in SQL?
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown: String functions in SQL allow you to manipulate text data in your queries. Common string functions include `UPPER()`, `LOWER()`, `LENGTH()`, and `SUBSTR()`. These functions can help you clean, format, or extract information from text columns. For example, you can use `UPPER(customer_name)` to display all customer names in uppercase.

sketch-2.title: Using string functions in SELECT
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
You can use string functions directly in the SELECT clause to create calculated columns. For example, to show the length of each customer's name in the `orders` table:

```sql
SELECT customer_name, LENGTH(customer_name) AS name_length FROM orders;
```
This will return each customer name along with the number of characters in their name.

sketch-3.title: Combining string functions and aliases
sketch-3.bodyMarkdown:
String functions are often combined with aliases to make results easier to read. For example, to display the first three letters of each region in uppercase:

```sql
SELECT region, UPPER(SUBSTR(region, 1, 3)) AS region_code FROM orders;
```
This query creates a new column `region_code` showing the first three letters of the region in uppercase.
